citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The primary enclosures in the outdoor housing area are not being sufficiently cleaned. A primary enclosure housing 2
adult Welsh Pembroke Corgis was observed with too numerous to count piles of excreta throughout the enclosure.
Additionally, a primary enclosure housing a single adult Welsh Pembroke Corgi was observed with too numerous to count
piles of excreta in the northeastern corner of the enclosure. The excreta observed in the primary enclosures varied in
appearance from fresh to frozen solid.
Failure to remove excreta from primary enclosures daily can result in an accumulation of feces which can lead to
increased disease hazards, discomfort for the animals, increased odors, and/or attract unwanted pests. Excreta and other
waste must be removed from primary enclosures daily to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces, to prevent soiling of
the dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors.
Correct by 18 February 2026

The facility does not have the names of treatments, nor the dates of administration recorded in the medical records. While
reviewing medical records for vaccines there was a 5-month-old Maltese female (Microchip #4092) found to not have a
current medical record for the administration of the 7-way vaccine. The program of veterinary care prescribes that juvenile
dogs should be administered a 7-way (DAPP/Leptospirosis) vaccine at 9, 12, and 15 weeks of age. When inquiring with
the facility representative on the vaccine status of this dog the representative stated the 7-way vaccines were
administered as prescribed, but he forgot to document it on a medical record.
Failure to record the names and dates for all treatments administered does not allow for APHIS officials to determine if
adequate veterinary care has been provided for the animals at the facility. The licensee must ensure that the names of all
vaccines and treatments administered, and the dates of administration are recorded and maintained for review by APHIS
officials.
Correct from this day 11 February 2026 and going forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.11(a) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The primary enclosures in the outdoor housing area are not being sufficiently cleaned. A primary enclosure housing 2 
adult Welsh Pembroke Corgis was observed with too numerous to count piles of excreta throughout the enclosure. 
Additionally, a primary enclosure housing a single adult Welsh Pembroke Corgi was observed with too numerous to count 
piles of excreta in the northeastern corner of the enclosure. The excreta observed in the primary enclosures varied in 
appearance from fresh to frozen solid.  
 
Failure to remove excreta from primary enclosures daily can result in an accumulation of feces which can lead to 
increased disease hazards, discomfort for the animals, increased odors, and/or attract unwanted pests. Excreta and other 
waste must be removed from primary enclosures daily to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces, to prevent soiling of 
the dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors.  
 
Correct by 18 February 2026 
3.13(b)(3) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The facility does not have the names of treatments, nor the dates of administration recorded in the medical records. While 
reviewing medical records for vaccines there was a 5-month-old Maltese female (Microchip #4092) found to not have a 
current medical record for the administration of the 7-way vaccine. The program of veterinary care prescribes that juvenile 
dogs should be administered a 7-way (DAPP/Leptospirosis) vaccine at 9, 12, and 15 weeks of age. When inquiring with 
the facility representative on the vaccine status of this dog the representative stated the 7-way vaccines were 
administered as prescribed, but he forgot to document it on a medical record.  
 
Failure to record the names and dates for all treatments administered does not allow for APHIS officials to determine if 
adequate veterinary care has been provided for the animals at the facility. The licensee must ensure that the names of all 
vaccines and treatments administered, and the dates of administration are recorded and maintained for review by APHIS 
officials.  
 
Correct from this day 11 February 2026 and going forward.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
 n
